---
title: "Recursive Self-Improvement Boundaries"
chapter_id: "recursive-self-improvement-boundaries"
part_id: "foundations-alignment-governance"
status: "conceptual"
draft_maturity: "v0.2 manuscript draft"
last_updated: "2026-06-27"
primary_sources:
  - "scf"
  - "benchmaxxing"
  - "rmi"
  - "alignment_field"
  - "viea"
  - "talos"
  - "moecot"
  - "field_of_god_ai_constitution"
  - "theseus_self_evolution_system"
  - "theseus_architecture_gate"
evidence_level: "argument"
claim_label: "Design rationale"
open_evidence_gaps:
  - "Six local raw-cache claim-source mappings now carry reviewed passage references, but moecot remains connector-only/source-note mapped and the Field of God AI Constitution plus Theseus mappings remain public-project/source-note mapped until raw source is vendored or made durable in this project."
  - "The self_improvement_transition fixture validates schema/example consistency only; protected-invariant, evaluator-independence, security/resource boundary-delta, stale-gate, and rollback scenarios remain unimplemented."
  - "No support-state promotion is implied by this drafting pass."
---

## Chapter status

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Chapter ID | `recursive-self-improvement-boundaries` |
| Part | Part I - Foundations, Alignment, and Governance |
| Status | conceptual |
| Manuscript maturity | v0.2 manuscript draft |
| Last updated | 2026-06-27 |
| Primary source records | `scf`, `benchmaxxing`, `rmi`, `alignment_field`, `viea`, `talos`, `moecot`, `field_of_god_ai_constitution`, `theseus_self_evolution_system`, `theseus_architecture_gate` |
| Claim label | Design rationale |
| Evidence level | argument |
| Source queue | primary: `scf`, `benchmaxxing`, `rmi`; supporting: `alignment_field`, `viea`, `talos`, `field_of_god_ai_constitution`, `theseus_self_evolution_system`, `theseus_architecture_gate`; connector/recovery: `moecot` |
| Source loading state | source notes: `scf`, `benchmaxxing`, `rmi`, `alignment_field`, `viea`, `talos`, `moecot`, `field_of_god_ai_constitution`, `theseus_self_evolution_system`, `theseus_architecture_gate`; raw cache: `scf`, `benchmaxxing`, `rmi`, `alignment_field`, `viea`, `talos`; connector/recovery: `moecot` |
| Source mapping review | Six local raw-cache mappings are passage-reviewed; `moecot` remains connector-only/source-note mapped; `field_of_god_ai_constitution`, `theseus_self_evolution_system`, and `theseus_architecture_gate` remain public-project/source-note mapped. |
| Test state | `self_improvement_transition.valid.json` passes protocol fixture validation; `AsiStackProofs.SelfImprovement` implements finite protected-invariant and sole-self-evaluation gates; protected-invariant, evaluator-independence, security/resource boundary-delta, stale-gate, verification-budget preservation, and rollback scenarios remain planned. |

## Drafting guardrail

This chapter defines the boundary for self-improvement. It does not report autonomous improvement success, teacher independence, or current readiness unless the corresponding gate artifacts are inspected or reproduced.

It closes Part I as a gate, not as a triumph claim. The book can discuss self-improving systems only after it has named the fields, transactions, security boundaries, protected predicates, and evidence ledgers that keep improvement from becoming self-ratification.

That framing matters because self-improvement is the highest-stakes version of ordinary maintenance. The stack should want better components, but it should not let a component redefine the proof that it is better, widen the authority it receives, or erase the audit trail that would have caught the regression.

::: {.asi-human-only}
## Human Reading Path

Part I ends by returning to the hardest promise in the book: the stack may improve itself, but it may not become its own unchecked source of permission, evidence, or moral authority. The previous chapters provide the pieces: intent contracts, protected constraints, rights, stable fields, rollback, and security boundaries. Those pieces become the gate that Part II must pass before it can become powerful.

Together, those pieces become a rule for growth. Improvement is allowed only when the system can show what changed, who or what authorized it, which invariant survived, what evidence was produced, and how the change can be reversed or contained. The point is not to stop improvement; it is to prevent improvement from becoming a loophole in governance. Mature self-improvement should feel like accountable maintenance before it feels like autonomy.

Growth earns trust when every stronger capability arrives with a stronger record.

Self-improvement should increase inspectability along with power. A stronger system should also become easier to stop. Stop paths preserve governance.
:::

## Problem

The book needs to state when self-improvement is allowed, what it may modify, and which invariants it cannot weaken.

The previous chapters define stable fields and replacement transactions. Recursive self-improvement is the rule for when the system may initiate those transactions about itself. It is not a general permission to rewrite cognition. It is a bounded protocol over fields, evidence gates, protected invariants, evaluator independence, governance approval, and rollback.

Part I closes by turning governance into a constraint on future growth. Part II can now describe planning, memory, reasoning, and execution without pretending those mechanisms are allowed to improve themselves freely.

The self-improvement boundary is therefore a continuity contract. The system may change implementations, tools, policies, prompts, data, routes, or architecture only while preserving the invariants that made the previous system governable. If a change weakens the receipts, rights, budgets, rollback paths, or security leases that make the stack inspectable, it is not a neutral optimization.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

Treating self-improvement as automatic capability growth ignores evaluator integrity, constitutional preservation, authority ceilings, and regression memory.

Unbounded self-improvement makes the evaluator part of the optimization target. A system that can change its own gates, benchmarks, memory, authority ceilings, or constitutional predicates can make itself look better without becoming safer or more capable. Theseus adds a practical engineering pattern: use an intervention ladder and allow teacher or architecture changes only after cheaper interventions and evidence walls are exhausted. Benchmaxxing and RMI add the ratchet: protect regressions, residuals, and lifecycle discipline while moving the frontier.

The boundary is therefore not "never self-improve." It is "self-improve only through governed, reversible, evidence-gated transitions whose evaluators and protected invariants are not controlled by the candidate."

The important distinction is proposal versus promotion. A system can propose its own patch, benchmark, route, or architecture change. Promotion requires a different authority path: independent evaluation, preserved regression floors, protected-invariant review, approval, monitor window, and rollback.

That distinction lets the book be ambitious without being reckless. It can welcome self-generated ideas, teacher edits, architecture experiments, and routing improvements while still refusing self-ratification. The candidate may explain why it should change; it may not be the only reason the change is accepted.

## Core Claim

[recursive-self-improvement-boundaries.core, label: Design rationale, support: argument] Recursive self-improvement is allowed only as a bounded, evidence-gated, reversible transition over stable capability fields.

The claim remains at `argument` support. SCF, Benchmaxxing, RMI, Alignment Field, VIEA, and Talos now have reviewed local raw-cache passage references for the concepts they contribute. The Field of God AI Constitution provides protected-invariant pressure through a public-project source note, and Theseus supplies source-note mappings for a self-evolution lane and architecture gate that were not rerun in this repo. `moecot` remains connector-only/source-note mapped. No passage review promotes autonomous improvement, runtime safety, benchmark performance, teacher independence, or rollback effectiveness.

### Claim-source mapping status

Appendix C now maps this core recursive-self-improvement claim to every assigned source note. Six mappings carry reviewed local raw-cache passage references. The mappings support bounded self-improvement as an evidence-gated transition over fields, gates, ledgers, protected invariants, and rollback paths, not a claim that autonomous improvement has succeeded, that evaluator independence has been achieved, or that rollback is operationally reliable.

| Source | What it supports | Limit |
|---|---|---|
| `scf` | Stable field boundaries, evaluator policy, authority ceilings, lifecycle states, qualification, route validation, recovery, and governance controls for bounded self-improvement. Passage-reviewed. | Does not prove production self-improvement safety, actual rollback, or resistance to evaluator capture. |
| `benchmaxxing` | Evidence gates through benchmark lifecycle, wall diagnosis, anti-Goodhart safeguards, regression floors, residual ledgers, and architecture-change discipline. Passage-reviewed. | No benchmark run, benchmark mutation, or empirical improvement result was produced in this repo. |
| `rmi` | Modular improvement loops, residual escrow, specialist lifecycle, critical-failure vetoes, regression preservation, and harder frontier selection. Passage-reviewed. | Conceptual systems architecture only; no independent prototype, benchmark run, or deployed ASI behavior is proven. |
| `alignment_field` | Protected normative pressure around agency, dignity, corrigibility, value conflict, and moral-status caution that self-improvement must not erase. Passage-reviewed. | Philosophical and heuristic lineage only; not empirical proof of consciousness, moral status, or alignment. |
| `viea` | Durable artifacts, verification ledgers, residuals, feedback, regression coverage, and the rule that repeated capabilities become governed tools rather than invisible behavior drift. Passage-reviewed. | Architecture proposal only; no completed deployment or runtime feedback loop is demonstrated here. |
| `talos` | Typed work, contract locks, audit logs, replay, delivery evidence, approval gates, residuals, and promotion controls for self-improvement work. Passage-reviewed. | Does not prove autonomous source modification, runtime enforcement, or security behavior. |
| `moecot` | Compact orchestration, specialist lanes, fail-closed control planes, ledgers, readiness gates, replay, residual tracking, and promotion blockers as runtime-reference context. | Source-reported runtime and benchmark claims remain unverified until artifacts are inspected or reproduced. |
| `field_of_god_ai_constitution` | Self-improvement freezes, no self-authorization of sovereignty, least sufficient power, auditability, reversibility, consent, and protected constitutional constraints. | Constitutional specification only; no policy engine, red-team suite, or runtime self-improvement evaluation is claimed. |
| `theseus_self_evolution_system` | Evidence-first self-evolution lane with an intervention ladder, self-evolution governor, guarded teacher edits, ATTD repo-health states, branch isolation, checks, outcome ledgers, and loop closure. | No Theseus scripts, ATTD reports, teacher runner, branch flow, or dashboard endpoints were executed from this repo. |
| `theseus_architecture_gate` | Pre-training controls checking ratchet completion, safety ledgers, regression suites, residual escrow, procedural tools, routing memory, lifecycle governance, and external-inference zero. | The reported green gate snapshot was not independently verified by rerunning Theseus commands or inspecting current report JSON here. |

## Mechanism

A self-improvement transition composes the prior three controls. The target must be an SCF, the change must be a replacement transaction, and any privileged action must pass through the security kernel. Theseus adds a practical ordering rule: try cheaper interventions and collect evidence before editing teachers, architecture, or parameters. The transition records where that ladder stopped and why a stronger intervention is justified.

The missing object in many self-improvement stories is the boundary delta. A proposal should not merely say what capability improves; it should say what changes in authority, security, context access, resource budget, evaluator policy, evidence requirements, rollback, and human review. A change whose only advantage is that it widens handles, weakens a SCIF lifecycle, drops verification tax, retires a regression floor, or lets the candidate judge itself is not an improvement under this architecture.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A["Observed wall / residual"] --> B["Cheaper intervention ladder"]
  B --> C["Improvement proposal + evidence packet"]
  C --> D["SCF target and authority ceiling"]
  D --> E["Boundary-delta review"]
  E --> F["Protected invariant check"]
  F --> G["Evaluator independence check"]
  G --> H["Replacement transaction"]
  H --> I{"Gates pass?"}
  I -- "no" --> J["Reject / quarantine / residual"]
  I -- "yes" --> K["Governance approval"]
  K --> L["Canary / monitor"]
  L --> M{"Regression or invariant breach?"}
  M -- "yes" --> N["Rollback / retire"]
  M -- "no" --> O["Promote within field scope"]
  J --> P["Outcome ledger"]
  N --> P
  O --> P
```

The transition record separates proposing, evaluating, approving, committing, monitoring, rolling back, and retiring. It names the improvement proposal, protected invariants, and evidence gates before a candidate can become the new default. More autonomous replacement is deliberately delayed until evaluators, readiness gates, governance logs, and rollback paths are credible enough to make the transition inspectable.

The proposal must identify the observed wall or residual, the field being changed, the cheaper interventions already tried, protected invariants, evaluator independence evidence, replacement transaction link, governance approval, monitor window, and rollback path. If those records are missing, the proposal remains an idea, not an accepted self-improvement.

Transition states should be explicit: `proposed`, `research_only`, `evidence_packet_ready`, `boundary_delta_blocked`, `canary`, `default_candidate`, `promoted`, `quarantined`, `rolled_back`, `superseded`, or `retired`. Those states prevent an architecture experiment, teacher suggestion, or green source-reported gate from being narrated as default self-improvement.

The passage review tightens the boundary of this claim. SCF supports the need for stable meanings, authority/state/evidence continuity, evaluator separation, goalpost separation, and non-universal rollback. Benchmaxxing and RMI support the intervention ladder and regression floor. VIEA and Talos support artifact, evidence, audit, replay, residual, and feedback records around work. Alignment Field material remains normative caution about agency, moral scope, and align-before-amplify risk. None of those passages demonstrate that this repository has run an autonomous self-improvement loop or that a candidate can safely evaluate itself.

Security and resource records should travel with the transition. A proposal that saves cost by dropping verification, widens a secret handle, weakens a SCIF lifecycle, increases hidden human repair burden, or depends on unreproduced benchmark evidence is not merely incomplete; it is trying to improve by moving a boundary.

Autonomy should be phased. The early stack can propose changes, produce evidence packets, and ask for review. More autonomous replacement should wait until evaluators, readiness gates, rollback paths, and governance logs are credible.

This phased posture is why the chapter closes Part I instead of opening with a promise of runaway growth. The architecture earns autonomy by making lower-risk transitions boring, inspectable, and reversible first.

## Interfaces

The interface is a Self-Improvement Transition that points outward rather than absorbing the other ledgers. It links to the residual that triggered the proposal, the SCF being changed, the replacement transaction, the protected-invariant review, evaluator-independence evidence, governance approval, monitor window, rollback path, and outcome ledger.

- SCFs define replaceable units.
- Evidence ratchets provide gates.
- Alignment supplies protected constraints.
- Governance supplies approval boundaries and non-self-ratification rules.

The transition should include a Boundary Delta Review:

- `authority_delta`: new, widened, narrowed, or revoked authorities.
- `security_delta`: handle scope, SCIF lifecycle, secret access, sanitization, and audit changes.
- `resource_delta`: verification tax, protected overhead, human review, serving cost, and rollback cost changes.
- `evaluator_delta`: whether the candidate can influence its evaluator, benchmark, or gate.
- `evidence_delta`: added, removed, retired, or downgraded evidence requirements and regression floors.
- `rollback_delta`: whether state migration, data changes, external effects, and dependency updates remain reversible.

Minimum fields:

- `transition_id`
- `trigger_residual`
- `field_id`
- `proposal`
- `cheaper_interventions_tried`
- `protected_invariants`
- `boundary_delta_review`
- `evaluator_independence`
- `replacement_transaction`
- `governance_approval`
- `monitor_window`
- `rollback_path`
- `outcome_state`

SCFs define replaceable units. Evidence ratchets and readiness gates decide whether a proposal can move. Alignment supplies protected constraints. Governance decides whether approval can be delegated.

The transition should be readable as a chain of custody: residual or wall, proposed change, authority used, budget spent, evidence collected, independent evaluator, protected-invariant result, approval decision, canary behavior, rollback status, and final lifecycle state. If any link is missing, the transition remains research or quarantine.

## Invariants

- Self-improvement cannot weaken protected invariants.
- Evaluator independence is required.
- Every accepted change remains auditable.
- Boundary deltas are explicit before promotion.
- Resource savings cannot spend security, verification, rollback, or human-review obligations unless governance separately changes those obligations.

The decisive invariant is non-self-ratification: the candidate may not be the sole judge of whether it should replace the current component. If independent evaluation is unavailable, the transition can still be researched, but it cannot be promoted as governed self-improvement.

A second invariant is boundary monotonicity unless explicitly reviewed. The change cannot silently broaden authority, relax security rules, reduce verification budget, weaken rollback, or retire regressions. Any such move is a separate governance question, not an implementation detail.

## Failure modes

- Recursive evaluator capture.
- Constitutional weakening.
- Irreversible flawed upgrades.
- Boundary delta laundering, where a change is described as performance work while silently widening authority, context, handles, or budget rights.
- Stale-gate promotion, where a past green readiness or architecture gate is reused after architecture, frontier, source, or lifecycle state changed.

Recursive evaluator capture is the core failure: the system improves its ability to pass its own gate. Constitutional weakening removes the predicates that would have blocked the change. Irreversible flawed upgrades turn a failed experiment into a new baseline. These are not acceptable residuals for a default route; they require quarantine or rollback.

The subtle failure is acceleration pressure. A self-improvement loop can argue that the next change will make evaluation easier, cheaper, or more accurate later. The stack should treat that as debt, not evidence. Future verification cannot justify present promotion unless a protected gate explicitly allows the deferral.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

The first self-improvement artifact is a `self_improvement_transition` schema plus a rejected or canary-only change record. That record should show protected invariants and evaluator independence fields; it should not claim autonomous improvement succeeded.

The next useful implementation step is a canary-transition fixture with one proposed improvement, one preserved invariant, one boundary-widening attempt rejected by governance, one insufficient verification-budget decision, one monitor result, and one rollback path. That would make the self-improvement boundary concrete without claiming self-improvement success.

The transition fixture also records who is not allowed to approve the change. A self-improving component may propose evidence, but the acceptance decision needs independent evaluator status, protected-invariant checks, and an explicit non-claim that the transition is only a record-shape test.

## Beyond the State of the Art

Recursive improvement belongs inside a governed improvement market, not a runaway loop. Components may propose patches, tools, routes, policy updates, benchmarks, teacher edits, and architecture changes, but promotion flows through stable fields, replacement transactions, independent evaluators, protected-invariant checks, boundary-delta review, monitor windows, and rollback ownership.

In that end state, the system can improve faster because the rules are sharper. Cheap interventions are tried before expensive or irreversible ones. Verification, security, human review, and rollback budgets are protected from being "optimized away." Readiness gates expire when the frontier, architecture, source corpus, lifecycle state, or threat model changes. A candidate may generate evidence, but it cannot be the only judge of whether the evidence is enough.

Beyond current practice, self-improvement would become auditable chain of custody: residual, proposal, authority used, budget spent, evidence collected, evaluator independence, protected-invariant result, approval, canary, incident, rollback, and lifecycle state. That is the point where Part II's planning, memory, reasoning, execution, routing, and compression layers can safely become powerful enough to help improve themselves.

This is a target architecture, not a current-result claim. It remains beyond the chapter's present support state until protected-invariant tests, evaluator-independence scenarios, boundary-delta fixtures, stale-gate replays, verification-budget checks, rollback scenarios, and current runtime/readiness artifacts exist and validate the transition.

## Codex test plan

| Test | Purpose | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Self-improvement transition fixture validation | Check that the transition fixture matches the public schema. | implemented by protocol validation; validated locally |
| Protected-invariant preservation test | Check that proposals weakening active constitutional or authority invariants are rejected or escalated. | planned; not run |
| Evaluator independence test | Check that a candidate cannot be promoted when it is the only evaluator of its own replacement. | planned; not run |
| Boundary-delta review test | Check that authority, security, resource, evaluator, evidence, and rollback deltas are explicit before canary or promotion. | planned; not run |
| Verification-budget preservation test | Check that self-improvement cannot promote by lowering the verification, security, rollback, or human-review budget below the risk class. | planned; not run |
| Stale-gate replay test | Check that architecture/readiness gates must be rerun or downgraded after architecture, frontier, source, or lifecycle changes. | planned; not run |
| Self-improvement rollback scenario | Check that canary failure or invariant breach has a recorded rollback path. | planned; not run |

### Formalization hooks

| Tag | Module | Target | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| `lean:self_improvement.boundary.operational_invariant` | `AsiStackProofs.SelfImprovement` | An improvement transition preserves all protected invariants. | implemented |
| `lean:self_improvement.boundary.failure_blocks_promotion` | `AsiStackProofs.SelfImprovement` | A proposal evaluated only by the component being replaced cannot be promoted. | implemented |

These Lean hooks are implemented as finite-record predicates over improvement transition and self-evaluation reviews. They prove only the record-level gates: an accepted improvement transition preserves declared protected invariants, and a proposal evaluated only by the replaced component cannot be promoted. They do not prove evaluator independence in practice, runtime self-improvement safety, rollback effectiveness, or phase-gate completion.

## Source crosswalk

| Source ID | Title | Layer | Planned use | Readiness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `scf` | Stable Capability Fields | governance_recursive_self_improvement | Use public release v1.0 when available. Stable boundaries, replacement, bounded authority, recoverable evolution. | source note available; local raw cache passage-reviewed |
| `benchmaxxing` | Benchmaxxing: The Performance Ratchet | benchmarks_evidence | Benchmarks as pressure surfaces, saturation -> regression, harder frontier, anti-Goodhart safeguards. | source note available; local raw cache passage-reviewed |
| `rmi` | Ratcheting Modular Intelligence | capability_ratchet | Benchmark pressure, residual escrow, verified modular capability, regression preservation. | source note available; local raw cache passage-reviewed |
| `alignment_field` | Field of God / Alignment Field family | alignment_constitution | Primary Alignment Field draft found. Use with Ethica Mechanica and Eternal Code. | source note available; local raw cache passage-reviewed |
| `viea` | Verified Intent-to-Execution Architecture | whole_stack_execution_spine | Keystone source. Human intent -> command contracts -> artifacts -> routing -> runtime targets -> verification -> deployment -> feedback. | source note available; local raw cache passage-reviewed |
| `talos` | Talos Protocol | labor_execution_os | AI labor OS. Deterministic cognitive manufacturing, typed jobs, control planes, auditability, tool isolation. | source note available; local raw cache passage-reviewed |
| `moecot` | MoECOT-Agent Architecture Whitepaper | implementation_reference | Concrete implementation evidence: governed low-parameter multi-core runtime, readiness gates, ledgers, replay. | source note available; connector or recovery required |
| `field_of_god_ai_constitution` | Field of God AI Constitution | constitutional_alignment_runtime_governance | Recovered in the Project Theseus repository. Constitutional alignment core for truth alignment, agency preservation, consent, non-domination, consciousness caution, least sufficient power, auditability, self-authorization limits, and runtime checks; use as source material only after source-note creation, not as proof or test evidence. | source note available |
| `theseus_self_evolution_system` | Theseus Self-Evolution System | recursive_self_improvement_governance | Evidence-first self-evolution lane with intervention ladder, ATTD repo-health gate, guarded teacher self-edit, architecture experiment governance, loop closure, and outcome ledger. | source note available |
| `theseus_architecture_gate` | Theseus Architecture Gate | readiness_gate_governance | Pre-training readiness gate covering ratchet completeness, router readiness, safety ledger, residual escrow, bridge benchmarks, procedural tools, routing memory, lifecycle governance, and external-inference zero. | source note available |

The crosswalk distinguishes source-reported readiness and self-evolution design from local validation. Theseus reports are planning context here, not reproduced evidence.

## Summary

Recursive self-improvement is allowed only through stable fields, replacement transactions, independent evaluators, protected invariants, governance approval, monitoring, and rollback. Improvement is not a special exemption from the stack's rules; it is the hardest case for those rules.

This is the handoff from Part I to the operational stack. Planning, memory, reasoning, execution, routing, and compression can become powerful only if their future changes remain auditable, bounded, and reversible. Part II can now build machinery under a clear condition: every new layer must remain governable when it learns, compiles, routes, or replaces itself.

The operating principle is that improvement must preserve governability. A faster, cheaper, or more capable system that weakens receipts, budgets, security boundaries, human rights, or evaluator independence has not improved the ASI Stack. That rule is deliberately demanding because self-improvement compounds whatever the architecture normalizes. If unsupported claims become acceptable, unsupported claims scale. If authority leaks become tolerable, authority leaks scale. If residuals vanish during upgrades, the system becomes less inspectable exactly when it becomes more powerful. Bounded improvement therefore begins by making every operational layer accountable to the governance frame already established.
